title: Banana bread
subtitle: The classic one-bowl loaf for very ripe bananas
excerpt: A moist, dense quick bread made with very ripe bananas, melted butter, and a touch of vanilla. It comes together in one bowl in ten minutes and bakes in an hour, needing no special skill or equipment.
sourceNotes: Banana bread entered the American baking canon during the 1930s when baking powder and baking soda became widely available household staples. The first widely circulated versions appeared in American community cookbooks and homemaker guides from the 1930s, 1940s. Pre-1928 versions exist in US newspaper archives. The recipe structure has changed little since: ripe bananas, fat, sugar, egg, flour, bicarbonate of soda.

content.text: This is the loaf for bananas you forgot about. The riper they are, the better: black-skinned and collapsed is ideal. Use
, anything less and the loaf lacks the sweetness and banana flavour that makes the recipe worth making. Three or four medium bananas gives about 300 g of mashed flesh.

Preheat the oven to 180°C conventional. Grease a 900 g loaf tin and line the base with a strip of baking paper. Mash the bananas well in a large bowl until smooth, with only small lumps remaining. Stir in the melted butter, then the sugar, beaten eggs, and vanilla. Sift over the flour, bicarbonate of soda, and salt. Fold together with a spatula until just combined; do not overwork. A few streaks of flour are fine. Overworked batter produces a tough, tunnelled loaf.

Pour the batter into the prepared tin and level the surface. Bake for 55 to 60 minutes until a skewer inserted through the centre comes out clean, the top is cracked and dark gold, and the loaf pulls slightly from the tin sides. If the top colours early, lay a sheet of foil loosely over the top for the last 15 minutes. Leave in the tin for 10 minutes before turning out onto a wire rack.
